Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted, narrating the words of the angels of revelation: **وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا** — "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that, and your Lord is not forgetful" (19:64). These verses indicate that the revelation, from the moment it begins its descent until it reaches the prophet and until he delivers it to the people, is preserved and protected from the alteration of any alterer.

These two modes of reasoning, although they are sufficient to establish the infallibility of the prophets (peace be upon them) in receiving the revelation and delivering the message only, rather than infallibility from sin in action as we have defined it, can have their indication completed to include infallibility from sin as well. This is because an act, like a word, is indicative according to rational people. The doer of an act indicates by his act that he sees it as good and permissible, just as if he had said: "Such an act is good and permissible." So if a sin were to occur from the prophet while he commands the opposite, that would be a contradiction from him, for his act would then contradict his word. He would then be a deliverer of two contradictory things, and the delivery of two contradictory things is not a delivery of the truth; for the one who reports two contradictory things has not reported the truth, since each of them invalidates the other. Thus, the prophet's infallibility in delivering his message is not complete except with his infallibility from sin and his protection from opposition, as is not hidden.

Their absolute infallibility is also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** — "They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance" (6:90). For all of them (peace be upon them) have had guidance ordained for them, and the Exalted has said: **وَمَن يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُ مِنْ هَادٍ وَمَن يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُ مِن مُّضِلٍّ** — "Whomever Allah leads astray has no guide, and whomever Allah guides, there is no one to lead him astray" (39:36-37).

And the Exalted said: **مَن يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ** — "Whomever Allah guides is the rightly guided" (18:17). He negated from those guided by His guidance every misleader who could affect them with misguidance (ضلال); thus, no misguidance is found in them. Every sin is misguidance, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ إِن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me. That is a straight path. Certainly he has led astray a great multitude of you’" (36:60-62). He counted every sin as misguidance occurring through the misleading of Satan after counting it as worship of Satan. Thus, establishing His guidance for the prophets (peace be upon them), then negating misguidance from those who are guided by His guidance, then counting every sin as misguidance, is a clearance from Him, the Exalted, of the station of His prophets from the commission of sin, as well as from the occurrence of error in their understanding of the revelation and their delivery of it.

It is also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ مع الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِين وَحَسُنَ أُولَٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا** — "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69). And He also said: **اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ** — "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray" (1:6-7). He described those whom He has blessed among the prophets as not being astray (الضالين). Had a sin been committed by them, they would have been astray thereby, and likewise had an error in understanding or delivery been committed by them. This meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in describing the prophets: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْرَائِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَاجْتَبَيْنَا إِذا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا** — "They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets of Adam’s progeny, and from those We carried with Noah, and from among the progeny of Abraham and Israel, and from among those We guided and chose. When the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down weeping in prostration. But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites. So they will soon encounter leeway" (19:58-59). He combined in the prophets two characteristics: blessing (الإنعام) and guidance (الهداية), where He used the explanatory "from" (مِن) in His saying "and from among those We guided and chose" after His saying "whom Allah has blessed." He described them with that which involves the utmost humility in servitude. Then He described the successors with the blameworthy traits mentioned. The second group is other than the first, because the first group are men who are praised and thanked, unlike the second. Since He described the second group and identified them as having followed their appetites and that they will encounter leeway (غياً), the first group—the prophets—did not follow their appetites nor did leeway (الغي) attach to them. It is self-evident that one whose state is this cannot have a sin committed by him; even if they were, before their prophethood, among those who followed appetites, they would have been among those to whom leeway attaches due to the absolute nature of His saying: "neglected the prayer and followed their appetites. So they will soon encounter leeway."

This mode of reasoning is close to the words of those who argue for the infallibility of the prophets through the intellect: that sending messengers and performing miracles at their hands is a confirmation of their words. Thus, no lie originates from them, and it is also a confirmation of their eligibility for delivery. The intellect does not consider a human from whom sins and acts contrary to the goal and purpose originate to be eligible to call toward that goal. Thus, the performance of miracles at their hands involves the confirmation of their lack of error in receiving the revelation, in delivering the message, and in their compliance with the obligations directed toward them through obedience.

The following objection is not valid: that people, being rational, use various types of delivery for their social purposes through those who are not free from some deficiency or negligence in delivery. For this occurs from them for one of two reasons that are impermissible in our context: either due to their tolerance of a small amount of deficiency and negligence, or because their purpose is to reach whatever is possible of the desired matter, grasping the little and overlooking the much. Neither of these two things is worthy of His station, the Exalted.

Nor is the following objection valid: the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَائِفَةٌ لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ وَلِيُنذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ** — "Why should not there go forth a group from every faction of them to learn the religion and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may beware?" (9:122). For although the verse is regarding the general Muslims who are not infallible, it gave them permission to deliver what they learned of the religion and understood of it; it did not confirm them in what they warned about nor did it establish their words as a binding proof (حجية) over the people. The problem lies only in the second case, not the first.

Among the things indicating their infallibility (peace be upon them) is His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ** — "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64), where He made the messenger being obeyed the goal of the sending, and restricted the goal to it. This necessitates, by clear implication, the attachment of His will, the Exalted, to everything in which the messenger is obeyed, which is his word or his act, because both are established and common means in delivery. So if an error in understanding the revelation or in delivery were realized from the messenger, that would be a will from Him, the Exalted, for falsehood, and Allah, the Glorified, only wills the truth.

Likewise, if a sin were to originate from the messenger in word or deed—and sin is hated and forbidden—it would be the very object of His will, the Exalted; thus, it would be the very same beloved obedience. He would then be willing and not willing, commanding and forbidding, loving and hating with respect to one and the same act—exalted is He far above the contradiction of attributes and acts. This is false even if we were to allow the obligation of the impossible (تكليف ما لا يطاق) as some have said; for the obligation of the impossible is an obligation of the impossible, whereas what we are discussing is the obligation itself being impossible, because it is an obligation and not an obligation, a will and not a will, love and not love, praise and blame with respect to one and the same act!

Among the things indicating this also is His saying, the Exalted: **رُّسُلًا مُّبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللَّهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ** — "apostles, as bearers of good news and as warners, so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles" (4:165). For the verse is apparent in that Allah, the Glorified, wants to cut off the excuse of the people in what involves opposition and sin, and that there is no cutter of the excuse except the messengers (peace be upon them). It is well known that the messengers' cutting off the people's excuse and their removal of their argument is only valid if nothing occurs from their side that does not agree with the will and pleasure of Allah: whether word or deed, error or sin. Otherwise, the people would have the right to hold onto it and argue against their Lord, the Glorified, which would be a frustration of His purpose, the Exalted.

**If you say:** What is indicated by the previous noble verses is that error does not occur from the prophets (peace be upon them) nor does sin originate from them, but this is not infallibility (العصمة) at all. For infallibility, according to what the people have mentioned, is a power (قوة) that prevents man from falling into error and restrains him from committing sin and perpetrating a mistake. Power is not merely the occurrence or non-occurrence of the act; rather, it is a psychological principle (مبدء نفساني) from which the act originates, just as acts originate from psychological traits (الملكات النفسانية).

**I say:** Yes, but what is needed in the previous discussions is the non-realization of error and sin from the prophet (peace be upon him), and it does not harm that the proof of a power from which the act originates as correct or as an obedience is not established, as is apparent.

Nevertheless, it is possible to argue that infallibility is based on a restraining power using what was mentioned in the discussion on miracles regarding the indication of His saying, the Exalted: "Allah carries through His command. Allah has ordained a measure for everything" (65:3), as well as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** — "Indeed my Lord is on a straight path" (11:56), that each of the events needs a principle from which it originates and a cause by which it is realized. So these acts originating from the prophet (peace be upon him) in a consistent manner as correct and as obedience trace back to a cause with the prophet (peace be upon him) and within himself, which is the restraining power. To clarify: the prophet's acts, assumed to originate as obedience, are voluntary acts (أفعال اختيارية) of the same type as the voluntary acts originating from us, some of which are obedience and some of which are sin. There is no doubt that a voluntary act is only voluntary by its originating from knowledge and will (المشية). The act only differs as obedience or sin by the difference of the mental image (الصورة العلمية) from which it originates. If the purpose is to proceed upon servitude by complying with the command, for example, obedience is realized. If the objective—meaning the mental image to which the will is added—is following desire and perpetrating what Allah has forbidden, sin is realized. Thus, the difference of our acts as obedience or sin is due to the difference of our knowledge from which the act originates. If one of the two knowledges—namely, the judgment of the obligation to proceed upon servitude and comply with the divine command—were to persist, nothing but obedience would originate. If the other knowledge from which sin originates were to persist (Allah forbid), nothing but sin would be realized. Accordingly, the origination of acts from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with the description of obedience always is only because the knowledge from which his act originates through will is a sound, unchanging mental image, which is the conviction of the obligation of servitude always. It is well known that a mental image and a psychological state that is rooted and non-vanishing is a psychological trait (الملكة النفسانية), like the trait of chastity, courage, justice, and the like. So in the prophet, there is a psychological trait from which his acts originate upon obedience and submission, and it is the power that restrains from sin.

From another perspective, the prophet does not err in receiving the revelation nor in delivering the message. So in him, there is a psychological state that does not err in receiving knowledge and delivering it, nor does it disobey in action. If we were to assume that these acts, while being in a consistent manner with nothing in them but correctness and obedience, were realized from him without the mediation of any of the causes that are with him, nor the addition of anything to the prophet's self, it would mean that his voluntary acts originate with that attribute by a will from Allah, the Glorified, without any involvement of the prophet (peace be upon him) in it. The necessary consequence of that would be the invalidation of the prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) knowledge and will in their influence on his acts. In that, voluntary acts would exit from being voluntary, which contradicts the assumption of his being an individual from among the human species who acts through knowledge and will. Thus, infallibility from Allah, the Glorified, is only by creating a cause in the human prophet from which his voluntary acts originate as correct and as obedience, and it is a type of rooted knowledge, which is the trait (الملكة) as previously mentioned.

## (A Discussion on Prophethood)

Allah, the Glorified, after mentioning this reality (which is the description of guiding people through revelation) many times in His speech, expressed its men with two different expressions, dividing them into two categories or something like a division: they are the Messenger (الرسول) and the Prophet (النبي). The Exalted said: **وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ** — "and the prophets and the witnesses shall be brought" (39:69). And the Exalted said: **يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَا أُجِبْتُمْ** — "The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’" (5:109). The meaning of Messenger is the bearer of a message (الرسالة), and the meaning of Prophet is the bearer of news (النبأ). Thus, the Messenger has the honor of mediation between Allah, the Glorified, and His creation, and the Prophet has the honor of knowledge of Allah and what is with Him.

It has been said that the difference between the Prophet and the Messenger is one of absolute generality and specificity (بالعموم والخصوص المطلق): the Messenger is the one who is sent and commanded to deliver and carries a message, while the Prophet is the one who is sent whether he is commanded to deliver or not.

However, this difference is not supported by His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ مُوسَىٰ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا** — "And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was dedicated, and he was an apostle and a prophet" (19:51). The verse is in the context of praise and arrangement, and it does not suit this context to progress from the specific to the general, as is not hidden.

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ** — "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you" (22:52), where He combined the Messenger and the Prophet in the speech and then made each of them "sent" (مرسلاً). However, His saying, the Exalted: "and the prophets and the witnesses shall be brought," as well as His saying, the Exalted: "but he is the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets," as well as what is in the verse under discussion: "Then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners," along with other verses, their apparent meaning gives that every one sent by Allah with a mission to the people is a prophet. This does not contradict what was previously mentioned of His saying, the Exalted: "and he was an apostle and a prophet," for the two terms were intended with their meanings without becoming names whose meanings are neglected; so the meaning is: "and he was a messenger expert in the signs of Allah and His knowledge." Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you," for it is possible to say: the Prophet and the Messenger are both sent to the people, except that the Prophet was sent to inform the people of the news of the Unseen that he has, due to his being expert in what is with Allah; and the Messenger is the one sent with a specific message in addition to the original news of prophethood, as suggested by such sayings of the Exalted: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْقِسْطِ** — "There is an apostle for every nation: when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them with justice" (10:47), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا** — "and We do not punish until We send an apostle" (17:15). Accordingly, the Prophet is the one who clarifies to the people the well-being of their worldly life and their return (معادهم) in terms of the principles of religion and its branches, according to what Allah's care necessitated of guiding people to their happiness. The Messenger is the bearer of a specific message containing the completion of proof (إتمام حجة) which, if opposed, is followed by destruction or punishment or the like. The Exalted said: "so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles." Nothing more appears from His speech, the Exalted, regarding the difference between them than what their two terms provide according to the concept (المفهوم). Its necessary consequence is what we pointed to: that the Messenger has the honor of mediation between Allah, the Exalted, and His servants, and the Prophet has the honor of knowledge of Allah and what is with Him. What has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding the difference between them will follow.

Furthermore, the Quran is explicit that the prophets are many and that Allah, the Glorified, did not narrate all of them in His Book. The Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ** — "Certainly We have sent apostles before you: of them are those We have recounted to you, and of them are those We have not recounted to you" (40:78), among others. Those whom Allah, the Exalted, narrated in His Book by name are twenty-some prophets, and they are: Adam, Noah, Idris, Hud, Salih, Abraham, Lot, Ishmael, Elisha, Dhul-Kifl, Elijah, Jonah, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Shu'ayb, Moses, Aaron, David, Solomon, Job, Zechariah, John, Ishmael the Truthful to the Promise, Jesus, and Muhammad—may Allah's blessings be upon them all.

There are several who were not mentioned by their names but by description and allusion. The Glorified said: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الْمَلَإِ مِن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ مِن بعد مُوسَىٰ إِذْ قَالُوا لِنَبِيٍّ لَّهُمُ ابْعَثْ لَنَا مَلِكًا** — "Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses, when they said to a prophet of theirs, ‘Appoint a king for us’" (2:246). And the Exalted said: **أَوْ كَالَّذِي مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ وَهِيَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا** — "Or like him who passed by a town as it lay fallen on its trellises" (2:259). And the Exalted said: **إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْهِمُ اثْنَيْنِ فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَعَزَّزْنَا بِثَالِثٍ** — "When We sent to them two [apostles], they impugned both of them. Then We reinforced them with a third" (36:14). And the Exalted said: **فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَا آتَيْنَاهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَاهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا** — "They found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves, and taught him a knowledge from Our own" (18:65). And the Exalted said: **وَالْأَسْبَاطِ** — "and the Tribes" (2:136). There are those whose status as a prophet is not clear, such as the youth of Moses in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِفَتَاهُ** — "When Moses said to his lad" (18:60), and like Dhul-Qarnayn, Imran the father of Mary, and Ezra (Uzayr) among those whose names are explicitly mentioned.

In short, no number is mentioned for them in the Quran at which they stop. Those narrations that contain a statement of their number are individual reports (آحاد) with differing texts, the most famous of which is the narration of Abu Dharr from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): that the prophets are one hundred and twenty-four thousand prophets, and the messengers among them are three hundred and thirteen prophets.

Know that the masters of the prophets are the "Possessors of Resolve" (أُولوا العزم) among them, and they are: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The Exalted said: **فَاصْبِرْ كَمَا صَبَرَ أُولُو الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ** — "So be patient just as the apostles of resolve were patient" (46:35). It will follow that the meaning of resolve (العزم) in them is steadfastness upon the first covenant taken from them and not forgetting it. The Exalted said: **وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثَاقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا** — "And when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge" (33:7). And the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا** — "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any resolve in him" (20:115).

Each one of these five is the possessor of a law (شرع) and a Book. The Exalted said: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus." And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَفِي الصُّحُفِ الْأُولَىٰ صُحُفِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ** — "This is indeed in the former scriptures, the scriptures of Abraham and Moses" (87:18-19). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَا التَّوْرَاةَ فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ** — "We sent down the Torah containing guidance and light. The prophets... would judge by it" (5:44), until He said: **وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰ آثَارِهِم بِعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ التَّوْرَاةِ وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًى وَنُورٌ** — "And We followed them with Jesus son of Mary, to confirm what was before him of the Torah, and We gave him the Evangel containing guidance and light" (5:46), until He said: **وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ فَاحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَاءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَاءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهَاجًا وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَلَٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي مَا آتَاكُمْ** — "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it. So judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires against the truth that has come to you. For each [community] among you We had appointed a code [of law] and a path, and had Allah wished He would have made you one community, but [His purposes required] that He may test you in what He has given you" (5:48).

The verses clarify that they have laws and that Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) have Books. As for the Book of Noah, you have already learned that the verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: "Mankind were a single community..." etc.—combined with His saying, the Exalted: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah," indicates it. This which we have mentioned does not contradict the revelation of the Book to David (peace be upon him). The Exalted said: **وَآتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ زَبُورًا** — "and We gave David the Psalms" (4:163). Nor does it contradict what is in the narrations regarding the attribution of books to Adam, Seth, and Idris, for they are books that do not contain rulings and laws.

Know that among the requirements of prophethood is revelation (الوحي), which is a type of divine speaking upon which prophethood depends. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ كَمَا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِن بَعْدِهِ** — "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163). The full discussion of its meaning will follow in Surah al-Shura, Allah willing.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) that he said: "Before Noah, people were a single community upon the primordial nature (فطرة) of Allah, neither guided nor astray, then Allah sent the prophets."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "That was before Noah." It was asked: "Were they upon guidance?" He replied: "Rather, they were astray. That was because when Adam passed away and his righteous progeny passed, and Seth his successor remained, he was unable to manifest the religion of Allah that Adam and his righteous progeny were upon. This was because Cain was threatening him with death just as he had killed his brother Abel. So he lived among them with dissimulation (التقية) and concealment, and they increased in misguidance every day until no one remained on earth with them except those who were predecessors. The successor joined an island in the sea to worship Allah. Then it appeared to Allah, Blessed and Exalted, to send the messengers. If these ignorant ones were asked, they would say the matter has been concluded, but they have lied. It is only a matter that Allah decrees every year." Then he recited: **فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ** — "In it is every wise matter distinct" (44:4). "So Allah, Blessed and Exalted, decrees what will be in that year of hardship or ease, rain or otherwise." I asked: "Were they astray before the prophets or upon guidance?" He said: "They were not upon guidance; they were upon the primordial nature (فطرة) of Allah upon which He fashioned them—there is no change in Allah's creation—and they were not to be guided until Allah guided them. Do you not hear the saying of Abraham: **لئن لم يهدني ربي لاكونن من الضالين** — 'If my Lord does not guide me, I will surely be among those who are astray' (6:77), meaning: forgetting the covenant."

**I say:** His saying: "They were not upon guidance; they were upon the primordial nature of Allah," explains the meaning of their being "astray" mentioned at the beginning of the hadith—that they were merely devoid of detailed guidance (الهداية التفصيلية) to divine knowledge. As for general guidance, it can coexist with being "astray" in the sense of ignorance of details, as indicated by his saying (peace be upon him) in the narration of *al-Majma'* quoted just now: "upon the primordial nature of Allah, neither guided nor astray."

And his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "meaning: forgetting the covenant," is an explanation of being "astray." Thus, guidance is the actual remembrance of the covenant, as is the case with the perfect among the believers, or proceeding in the state of one who remembers the covenant even if he does not actually remember it, which is the state of the rest of the believers. The application of "guidance" to the latter is not without a certain figurative nuance.

In *al-Tawhid*, narrated from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: "The heretic who came to Abu Abdillah asked: 'From where do you prove prophets and messengers?' Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'When we have proven that we have a Creator, a Maker, exalted above us and above all that He has created, and that Maker was Wise, it was not permissible for His creation to witness Him, nor touch Him, nor for Him to have direct contact with them or they with Him, nor for Him to argue with them or they with Him. Thus, it was proven that He has ambassadors among His creation who direct them to their interests and benefits and that in which lies their survival, and in the abandonment of which lies their destruction. Thus, the commanders and forbidders from the Wise, the All-Knowing, were proven among His creation. It was proven at that point that He has spokesmen, and they are the prophets and His elite from His creation, wise men disciplined with wisdom, sent with it, not sharing with the people in their states despite sharing with them in creation and composition, supported from the Wise, the All-Knowing, with wisdom and proofs, evidences and witnesses, such as reviving the dead and healing the blind and the leper. Thus, the earth of Allah is never devoid of a Proof (حجة) who possesses knowledge that indicates the truth of the messenger's speech and the necessity of his justice.'"

**I say:** The hadith, as you see, contains three arguments regarding three issues of prophethood.

**One of them:** The argument for general prophethood. Upon reflection on what he (peace be upon him) mentioned, you will find that it corresponds to what we derived from His saying, the Exalted: "Mankind were a single community..." the verse.

**Second:** The proof for the necessity of supporting the Prophet with a miracle (المعجزة). What he (peace be upon him) mentioned aligns with what we discussed regarding the investigation of inimitability (الإعجاز) in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِ** — "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (Al-Baqarah: 23).

**Third:** The issue of the earth never being devoid of a Proof (الحجة) — *the divine representative who acts as a decisive authority between Allah and His creation*; its explanation will follow, if Allah wills.

In *al-Ma’ani* and *al-Khisal*, narrated from ‘Utbah al-Laythi, from Abu Dharr (may Allah have mercy on him), who said: I said, "O Messenger of Allah, how many were the prophets (النبيون)?" He said, "One hundred and twenty-four thousand prophets." I said, "How many were the messengers (المرسلون) among them?" He said, "Three hundred and thirteen—a vast multitude." I said, "Who was the first of the prophets?" He said, "Adam." I said, "And was he a messenger among the prophets?" He said, "Yes; Allah created him with His hand and breathed into him of His spirit." Then he said, "O Abu Dharr, four of the prophets were Syriac: Adam, Seth (شيث), Enoch (أخنوخ)—who is Idris and was the first to write with the pen—and Noah. And four were from the Arabs: Hud, Salih, Shu’ayb, and your prophet, Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. The first prophet of the Children of Israel was Moses and the last of them was Jesus, and [between them were] six hundred prophets." I said, "O Messenger of Allah, how many books did Allah, the Exalted, send down?" He said, "One hundred and four books. Allah sent down fifty scrolls (صحيفة) to Seth, thirty scrolls to Idris, and twenty scrolls to Abraham; and He sent down the Torah (التوراة), the Gospel (الإنجيل), the Psalms (الزبور), and the Criterion (الفرقان)."

**I say:** This narration—especially its beginning concerning the number of prophets and messengers—is among the well-known reports (المشهورات) narrated by both the elite (الخاصة) — *the Shi'a scholars* — and the general public (العامة) — *the Sunni scholars* — in their books. Al-Saduq narrated this meaning in *al-Khisal* and *al-Amali* from al-Rida, from his forefathers, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم; and from Zayd ibn ‘Ali, from his forefathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام). It was also narrated by Ibn Qulawayh in *Kamil al-Ziyarat*, by al-Sayyid [ibn Tawus] in *al-Iqbal* from al-Sajjad (عليه‌السلام), and in *al-Basa’ir* from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا** — "and he was a messenger, a prophet" (Maryam: 51), he said: "The prophet (النبي) is he who sees in his sleep and hears the voice but does not behold the angel; and the messenger (الرسول) is he who hears the voice, does not see in sleep, and beholds [the angel]."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. It is possible to derive this from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَارُونَ** — "so send for Aaron" (Ash-Shu'ara: 13). This does not mean that the definition of a messenger is simply one to whom the angel of revelation is sent; rather, the intent is that prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة) are two distinct stations (مقامان). The characteristic of one is the vision (الرؤيا), and the characteristic of the other is witnessing the angel of revelation. Sometimes both stations combine in one individual, thus combining both characteristics. At other times, there may be prophethood without messengership. Thus, messengership is more specific than prophethood in terms of its external instances (مصداقاً), not its conceptual meaning (مفهوماً), as explicitly stated in the previous hadith from Abu Dharr where he asks: "How many were the messengers among them?"

It has thus become clear that every messenger is a prophet, but not vice versa. Through this, the answer appears to the objection raised by some regarding the indication of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَٰكِن رَّسُولَ اللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ** — "but [he is] the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets" (Al-Ahzab: 40). They argued that it only indicates the sealing of prophethood, not the sealing of messengership, citing this narration and its likes as evidence.

The answer is: Prophethood is more general in its instances than messengership, and the cessation of the more general necessitates the cessation of the more specific. There is no indication in the narrations, as you have seen, of a general-particular relationship from one aspect (العموم من وجه) between messengership and prophethood; rather, the narrations are explicit in their absolute generality (العموم المطلق).

In *al-Uyun*, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام), he said: "The 'Possessors of Resolve' (أُولوا العزم) were only named so because they were masters of firm determination (العزائم) and divine laws (الشرائع). This is because every prophet after Noah was upon his law and way (منهاجه) and a follower of his book until the time of Abraham, the Friend. Every prophet in the days of Abraham was upon the law of Abraham and his way and a follower of his book until the time of Moses. Every prophet in the time of Moses was upon the law of Moses and his way and a follower of his book until the days of Jesus. Every prophet in the days of Jesus and after him was upon the law of Jesus and his way and a follower of his book until the time of our prophet Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. These five are the 'Possessors of Resolve'; they are the most excellent of the prophets and messengers (عليهم السلام). The law of Muhammad shall not be abrogated until the Day of Resurrection, and there is no prophet after him until the Day of Resurrection. Whoever claims prophethood after him or brings a book after the Qur'an, his blood is lawful for anyone who hears that from him."

**I say:** The author of *Qisas al-Anbiya* narrated this meaning from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَاصْبِرْ كَمَا صَبَرَ أُولُو الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ** — "So be patient just as the possessors of resolve among the apostles were patient" (Al-Ahqaf: 35), [it is said]: They are Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus son of Mary (عليهم السلام). The meaning of 'Possessors of Resolve' is that they preceded the [other] prophets in acknowledging Allah, and they acknowledged every prophet before them and after them, and they resolved upon patience despite being denied and harmed.

**I say:** It has been narrated through the channels of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah* from Ibn Abbas and Qatadah that the 'Possessors of Resolve' among the prophets are five: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, just as we narrated from the channels of the *Ahl al-Bayt*. There are other opinions attributed to some: some held they are six: Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, and Job. Others held they are those who were commanded with struggle (الجهاد) and fighting, who manifested open confrontation and strove for the religion. Others held they are four: Abraham, Noah, Hud, and the fourth is Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. These are opinions devoid of proof, and we have already mentioned the correct view in this regard.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from al-Thumali, from Abu Ja’far (عليه‌السلام), he said: "Between Adam and Noah, the prophets were hidden (مستخفين); therefore, their mention is hidden in the Qur'an and they were not named as the manifest (استعلن) prophets were named..." [to the end of the hadith].

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated from the People of the House of Infallibility (عليهم السلام) through numerous channels.

In *al-Safi*, from *al-Majma’*, from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام): "Allah sent a black prophet whose story He did not relate to us."

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*, he (عليه‌السلام) said in a sermon mentioning Adam (عليه‌السلام): "Then He sent him down to the abode of trial and the procreation of progeny. And He, the Glorified, chose from his offspring prophets for whom He took their covenant (ميثاقهم) upon revelation, and their trust (أمانتهم) upon the delivery of the message, when most of His creation had changed the covenant of Allah to them, becoming ignorant of His right and taking peers (الأنداد) alongside Him. The devils turned them away from knowing Him and cut them off from His worship (عبادته). So He sent among them His messengers and followed them with His prophets in succession (واتر), to demand from them the fulfillment of the covenant of His primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and to remind them of His forgotten blessings, and to establish the proof against them through delivery [of the message], and to unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects (العقول), and to show them the signs of [His] power: from a roof raised above them, and a cradle placed beneath them, and livelihoods that sustain them, and terms (آجال) that terminate them, and ailments (أوصاب) that age them, and events (أحداث) that follow one another upon them. Allah, the Glorified, never left His creation without a sent prophet, or a revealed book, or a binding proof (حجة لازمة), or an established path (محجة قائمة). [They were] messengers who were not deterred by the smallness of their number nor the greatness of those who denied them: from a predecessor who named the one after him, or a successor who was introduced by the one before him. Upon this, the generations passed (نسلت), the ages went by, the fathers departed, and the sons succeeded them, until Allah, the Glorified, sent Muhammad to fulfill His promise and complete His prophethood..." [the sermon].

**I say:** His saying "اجتالتهم" means they caused them to wander (الجولان) in every direction. His saying "واتر اليهم" means He sent one after another. "الأوصاب" is the plural of "وصب", which is illness. "الأحداث" is the plural of "الحدث", which is a calamity. His saying "نسلت القرون" means they passed away. "إنجاز العدة" is the fulfillment of the promise, referring to the promise Allah, the Glorified, made to send His messenger Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, which was heralded by Jesus (عليه‌السلام) and other prophets (عليهم السلام). He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا** — "The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice" (Al-An'am: 115).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abdullah ibn al-Walid, who said: Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) said: "Allah, the Exalted, said to Moses (عليه‌السلام): **وَكَتَبْنَا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْوَاحِ مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — 'And We wrote for him in the tablets [something] of everything' (Al-A'raf: 145); thus we know that He did not write everything for Moses. And He, the Exalted, said to Jesus: **لِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ** — 'so that I may clarify for you some of that on which you differ' (Az-Zukhruf: 63). And Allah, the Exalted, said to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: **وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَٰؤُلَاءِ شَهِيدًا وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — 'and We shall bring you as a witness against these. We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things' (An-Nahl: 89)."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in *Basa’ir al-Darajat* from Abdullah ibn al-Walid through two channels. His saying (عليه‌السلام) "Allah said to Moses..." is an indication that His saying, the Exalted, "in the tablets [something] of everything" (في الالواح من كل شيء) explains His saying regarding the Torah: "and a detailed explanation of all things" (وتفصيل كل شيء). For if the intent were an exhaustive clarification of all aspects of everything, it would not be correct to say "of (من) everything" in the tablets. Thus, this speech is a witness that the intent of "detailed explanation of all things" is its explanation in a certain respect, not from all aspects. So understand this.

## (Philosophical Discussion)

The issue of general prophethood (النبوة العامة) — *the universal necessity of divine guidance through prophets* — from the perspective of prophethood being a mode of delivering laws and established legislated rules, involves conventional matters (أُمور اعتبارية) rather than ontological realities (حقيقية). As such, it is a theological (كلامية) rather than a philosophical issue, for philosophical inquiry only deals with things in terms of their external existence (وجوداتها الخارجية) and their concrete realities (حقائقها العينية), and does not encompass legislated, conventional matters.

However, from another perspective, it is a philosophical issue and a study of reality. This is because the religious materials—comprising primary knowledge (المعارف الاصلية) and moral and practical laws—have a connection to the human soul (النفس الانسانية) in that they establish within it firmly rooted knowledge or states that lead to ingrained dispositions (ملكات راسخة). This knowledge and these dispositions become forms (صور) for the human soul that determine its path toward felicity (السعادة) or wretchedness (الشقاوة), and proximity to or distance from Allah, the Glorified. For through righteous deeds (الاعمال الصالحة) and true, sincere beliefs, man acquires for his soul perfections that relate only to what has been prepared for him with Allah of proximity, station, pleasure, and gardens. Conversely, through evil deeds and absurd, false beliefs, he acquires for his soul forms that relate only to the perishing world and its fleeting ornaments, leading him—after departing the world and the cessation of choice—to the abode of ruin and the bed of fire. This is a real journey (سير حقيقي).

Accordingly, the issue is one of reality, and the proof we mentioned in the previous commentary, derived from the Mighty Book, is a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية).

To clarify: these forms of the human soul occur in the path of perfection (الاستكمال). Man is a real species (نوع حقيقي), meaning he is a real existent that is the source of concrete existential effects. The overflowing causes (العلل الفياضة) of existents have given them the capacity to attain their ultimate perfection in their existence, as attested by experience and demonstration. The Necessary Being (الواجب تعالى) is perfect in His emanation (الافاضة); thus, there must be an emanation for every soul that is prepared, according to what suits its preparation of perfection, by which its potentiality is transformed into actuality. This perfection is called felicity if the soul possesses good attributes and virtuous, balanced dispositions, or it is called wretchedness if it possesses vices and base forms.

Since these dispositions and forms are acquired through voluntary actions (الافعال الاختيارية) arising from the belief in benefit or harm, fear and hope, desire for gains, and dread of losses, it is necessary that this emanation also relates to the religious call (الدعوة الدينية) through glad tidings, warning, intimidation, and enticement. This serves as a healing for the believers so they may be perfected in their felicity, and as a loss for the wrongdoers (الظالمين) so they may be perfected in their wretchedness. The call requires a caller to present it, who is the Prophet sent from Him, the Exalted.

**If you say:** The call of the intellect (العقل) is sufficient, as it calls man to follow the truth in belief and action, and to tread the path of virtue and godwariness (التقوى). What need is there for the sending of prophets?

**I say:** The intellect that calls to this and commands it is the practical intellect (العقل العملي) — *the faculty that judges the goodness or badness of actions* — which judges beauty and ugliness, not the theoretical intellect (العقل النظري) — *the faculty that perceives the realities of things* — as previously explained. The practical intellect takes the premises of its judgment from internal sensations. The sensations that are actually present in man in his initial state are the sensations of the appetitive and irascible powers. As for the holy rational power (القوة الناطقة القدسية), it is only in potentiality. It has already been mentioned that this primordial sensation calls toward discord. Thus, these [powers] which are in actuality do not allow man to emerge from potentiality to actuality, as is witnessed in the human condition. Any people or individual who lacks sound upbringing soon reverts to savagery and barbarism despite the presence of intellect within them and the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon them. Thus, there is no dispensing with divine support through a prophethood that reinforces the intellect.

## (Social Discussion)

**If you say:** Granting that the intellect is not independent in action for every individual or every people in all circumstances, yet nature (الطبيعة) always inclines toward what contains its welfare. Society, which follows nature, similarly guides toward the welfare of its members. It eventually settles upon a sound form containing the felicity of the members of society—this is the known principle of being influenced by the environment. The interaction between opposing sides eventually leads to a sound society suitable for the environment of human life, bringing about the felicity of the species whose individuals are gathered. This is attested by what we observe and supported by history: that societies continue to incline toward perfection, desire reform, and head toward the felicity that is pleasurable to man. Some have reached their goal and desire, as in some nations like Switzerland, and some are on the way, having not yet completed the conditions of perfection, whether they are near or far, as in other states.

**I say:** The inclination of nature toward its perfection and felicity is something no one can deny. Society, which terminates at nature, is like nature in its orientation toward perfection. However, what should be carefully considered is that this inclination and orientation do not necessitate the actuality of perfection and true felicity. This is because of what we mentioned regarding the actuality of appetitive and irascible perfection in man, while the principles of true felicity in him are in potentiality. The proof for this is the very thing cited in the objection: that civil societies, both past and present, are oriented toward perfection, with some attaining a virtuous and felicitous civilization, and others being near or far. For the perfection and felicity we find among these is physical perfection (الكمال الجسمي), and physical perfection is not the perfection of man. Man is not merely the body; rather, he is a composite of body and spirit, comprised of two aspects: material (مادية) and spiritual (معنوية). He has a life in the body and a life after departing it without annihilation or cessation. He requires a perfection and felicity upon which he can rely in his afterlife. Thus, it is not correct to consider his physical perfection, based on natural life, as his [ultimate] perfection and felicity, given that his reality is this [dual] reality.

It has thus become clear that society, according to experience, only actually moves toward the actuality of physical perfection rather than the actuality of human perfection. Even if its intent is to guide man to the perfection of his reality, not the perfection of his body—which, by strengthening its side, leads to the destruction of humanity, the dissolution of its composition, and its straying from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — this perfection cannot be completed for him except through the support of prophethood and divine guidance.

**If you say:** If this prophetic claim were true and linked to existential guidance (الهداية التكوينية), its necessary consequence would be the actuality of its effect in human societies, just as the guidance of man—indeed, every created being—toward the benefits of its existence is an actual matter proceeding in creation and existence. Thus, it would be necessary for societies to be clothed in it, and for it to proceed among people like other proceeding instincts. But this is not the case. How then can it be a true reform when human societies do not accept it? The religious call in its removal of the discords of life is nothing but a hypothesis incapable of being applied to reality.

**I say:** Firstly, the effect of the religious call is witnessed and observed; none but a stubborn denier would doubt it. For in all the ages of its existence since it appeared, it has raised thousands upon thousands of individuals on the side of felicity, and many times that number on the side of wretchedness through acceptance and rejection, submission and arrogance, faith and disbelief. This is in addition to some religious societies that have formed at certain times. Furthermore, the world has not yet finished its lifespan, and the human world has not yet perished. It is possible that human society will one day transform into a sound religious society, containing true human life and the felicity of high virtues and morals—a day when none but Allah, the Glorified, is worshipped, and where justice and virtue are the path. It is not permissible to consider such a great effect as trivial or negligible.

**Secondly:** Social studies, as well as psychology and ethics, prove that actions realized externally are linked to states and dispositions of character; they "suckle from the breast" of psychological attributes and have an effect on souls. Actions are the effects of souls and their attributes, and they have effects back upon the souls and their attributes. From this, two principles are deduced: the principle of the contagion (سراية) of attributes and morals, and the principle of their inheritance (وراثة). They expand in existence through contagion horizontally, and expand through the persistence of their existence via inheritance vertically.

This great call, accompanying human societies from their earliest eras—in recorded history and before it—must have a deep effect on human social life in terms of virtuous morals and noble, generous attributes. Thus, the religious call has effects on souls even if they do not respond to it or believe in it.

Rather, the reality of the matter is that what we observe in present societies among living nations are the effects of prophethood and religion, which they have possessed through inheritance or imitation. For since religion appeared among this species, it was carried and adopted by significant nations and groups. It is the sole caller that invites to faith, virtuous morals, justice, and reform. Thus, the praiseworthy traits found among people today, even if few, are remnants of its effects and results. For the general modes of governance in formed societies are three, with no fourth: the first is autocratic governance (تدبير الاستبداد), which leads to enslavement in all human affairs; the second is civil laws, which proceed and judge in actions only, calling for freedom in what lies beyond that of morals and other things; and the third is religion, which judges in beliefs, morals, and actions altogether, calling for the reform of all.

If there is any hoped-for good or felicity in the world, it must be attributed to religion and its upbringing.

This is attested by what we observe of the nations that built their society upon the perfection of nature and neglected the matter of religion and morals. They did not last long before they lost reform, mercy, love, purity of heart, and other moral and primordial virtues, despite the presence of the root of the primordial nature (الفطرة) within them. If the root of the primordial nature were sufficient, and these attributes were not among the inherited remnants of religion among mankind, they would not have lost any of that.

Furthermore, history is the truest witness to the borrowings made by Christian nations after the Crusades; they borrowed the essential points from general Islamic laws, adopted them, and advanced through them. Meanwhile, the Muslims cast them behind their backs; thus the latter regressed and the former advanced—and this is a lengthy discussion.

In short, the two mentioned principles—namely contagion and inheritance, which are the instinctive imitation in man and the preservation of familiar conduct—necessitate the penetration of the religious spirit into societies just as they necessitate it in other things, and this is an actual effect.

**If you say:** Based on this, what is the use of the primordial nature (الفطرة), for it seems to avail nothing, and the matter of felicity is solely in the hand of prophethood? And what is the use of building legislation upon the foundation of the primordial nature, as prophethood claims?

**I say:** What we previously presented in explaining the connection of the primordial nature to human felicity and perfection is sufficient to resolve this doubt. For the felicity and perfection that prophethood brings to man is not something external to this species, nor is it foreign to the primordial nature. For it is the primordial nature that is guided to it, but this guidance is not completed for it actually by itself without a helper to assist it. This helper that assists it—which is the reality of prophethood—is also not something external to humanity and its perfection, joined to man like a stone placed beside a man, for example. Otherwise, what would return from it to man would be something other than his perfection and felicity, like the weight that a stone adds to the weight of a man. Rather, it is also a primordial perfection for man, stored within this species. It is a special consciousness (شعور خاص) and a specific perception hidden in his reality, to which none are actually guided except individuals of the species who have been encompassed by divine care. This is just as a mature human has a special consciousness of the pleasure of marriage, to which the rest of the immature individuals are not actually guided, even though all—the mature and the immature—share in the human primordial nature, and the consciousness is a consciousness linked to that nature.

In short, neither is the reality of prophethood something additional to the humanity of the human who is called a prophet, nor is it external to his primordial nature; nor is the felicity to which the rest of the community is guided something external to their humanity and primordial nature, foreign to what their human existence finds familiar. Otherwise, it would not be perfection and felicity in relation to them.

**If you say:** Then the objection returns, according to this report, to prophethood; for the primordial nature on this basis is sufficient by itself, and prophethood is not outside of the primordial nature.

The result of this speech is that the human species, which is civilized by its primordial nature and discordant in its society, is distinguished by having among its individuals certain righteous ones whose primordial nature is upright and whose intellects are sound from delusions, whims, and base attributes. They are guided by the uprightness of their nature and the soundness of their intellects to what contains the reform of society and the felicity of man. Thus, they lay down laws containing the welfare of the people and the prosperity of this world and the hereafter. Thus, the prophet is the righteous man who possesses social genius (نبوغ اجتماعي).

**I say:** Not at all! This is an interpretation that does not apply to the reality of prophethood, nor to what it entails.

**Firstly:** Because this is a hypothesis assumed by some social scientists who have no footing in religious inquiry or the investigation of the realities of the Origin and the Return (المبدء والمعاد).

They mentioned that prophethood is a special social genius resulting from the uprightness of the primordial nature and the soundness of the intellect. This genius calls for reflection on the state of society, what reforms this disordered society, and what brings felicity to social man.

Thus, this social genius is the prophet; the sound thought emanating from his intellectual powers is the revelation (الوحي); the laws he establishes for the reform of society are the religion (الدين); his pure soul that emanates these thoughts to his intellectual powers and does not betray the human world by following whims is the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الامين), which is Gabriel; the true Revealer is Allah, the Glorified; the book that contains his high, pure thoughts is the Heavenly Book; the angels (الملائكة) are the natural powers or the factors calling toward good; and the devil (الشيطان) is the soul that commands to evil (النفس الامارة بالسوء) or the powers or factors calling toward evil and corruption—and so on.

This is a false hypothesis. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on inimitability (الإعجاز) that prophethood in this sense is more fittingly called a political game than a divine prophethood.

It has also preceded that this thought—which these researchers call a special genius and name prophethood—is among the properties of the practical intellect, which distinguishes between the good and evil of actions through benefit and harm. This is a matter shared among the rational ones of the human species and is among the gifts of the shared primordial nature. It has also preceded that this very intellect is the caller to discord. If this is its state, it cannot, insofar as it is such, remove discord; it requires a completer to complete its affair. You have learned that this completer must be a special type of consciousness (شعور) that is specific in its actuality to some individuals of mankind, through which the primordial nature is guided to the true felicity of man in his worldly life and his return.

From here it appears that this consciousness is not of the same genus as intellectual consciousness (الشعور الفكري). This means that what man finds of intellectual results through the path of his rational premises is different from what he finds through the path of prophetic consciousness; the path is different from the path.

Researchers of the properties of the soul do not doubt that in man there is an internal psychological consciousness that sometimes appears in some individuals, opening for them a door to a world beyond this world, and giving them wonders of knowledge and information beyond what the intellect and thought can attain. This has been stated by all the psychologists among our predecessors and a group of psychologists from Europe, such as the Englishman [William] James and others.

It has thus been established that the door of prophetic revelation is other than the door of rational thought, and that prophethood, as well as the divine law (الشريعة), religion, the book, the angel, and the devil, do not correspond to the meanings they have fabricated.

**Secondly:** Because the transmitted speech of these prophets who claimed the station of prophethood and revelation—such as Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, Jesus, Moses, Abraham, Noah (عليه السلام), and others, some of whom confirm others—as well as what exists of their books like the Qur'an, is explicit in contradicting this interpretation they have given for prophethood, revelation, the descent of the book, the angel, and other realities. For the explicit text of the Book and the Sunnah, and what has been transmitted from the great prophets (عليهم السلام), is that these realities and their effects are matters outside the genus of nature, the realm of matter, and the judgment of the senses, such that reducing them to nature and its judgment is considered nothing but an interpretation (تأويل) that the nature of speech does not accept and the taste of discourse does not approve.

It has become clear from what we mentioned that the matter which removes the corruption of discord from human society—which is the internal consciousness that perceives the reform of society, meaning the power by which the prophet is distinguished from others—is a matter beyond the intellectual consciousness shared by all rational individuals of mankind.

**If you say:** Based on this, this internal consciousness would be a matter that breaks the norm (خارقاً للعادة), for it is something that human individuals do not know of themselves; rather, it is something claimed by the rare few among them. How then can it lead everyone to the reform of their affairs and guide the species to its true felicity?! It has already been mentioned that whatever is assumed as a guide for man to his species-specific felicity and perfection must guide him through connection and union with his primordial nature, not in the manner of an addition like a stone placed beside a man.

**I say:** That this matter breaks the norm is beyond doubt. Likewise, that it is of the category of internal perceptions and a type of consciousness hidden from the external senses is beyond doubt. However, the intellect does not reject a matter that breaks the norm nor a matter hidden from the external senses; it only rejects the impossible (المحال). The intellect has a way to verify matters that break the norm and are hidden from the external senses, for it can argue for a thing through its causes—which is the *a priori* (لمي) deduction—or through its concomitants or effects—which is the *a posteriori* (إني) deduction—thereby establishing its existence. Prophethood in the sense we mentioned can be argued for through one of two ways: sometimes through its effects and consequences, which is the inclusion of the religion brought by the prophet of the felicity of man in his world and his hereafter; and sometimes from the perspective of concomitants. This is because when prophethood is a matter that breaks the norm, the claim of the one who claims it is a claim that what is beyond nature—which is its God who guides it to its felicity and guides the human species to its perfection and felicity—acts upon some individuals of the species in a way that breaks the norm, which is the act of revelation. If this act that breaks the norm were permissible, others like it would be permissible, for the judgment of likes in what is permissible and what is not is one. Thus, if the claim of prophethood from the prophet were true and the prophet possessed it, it would be permissible for him to bring another matter that breaks the norm, linked to his prophethood in a way that necessitates the conviction of the intellect that doubts the prophethood of this claimant. This matter that breaks the norm is the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة). We have discussed inimitability (الإعجاز) in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِ** — "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (Al-Baqarah: 23).

**If you say:** Granting that this discord is removed by this internal consciousness called prophetic revelation and the prophet established it through miracles, and it was upon the people to accept its effects—which is the legislated religion brought by the prophet—but what is the safeguard against error? And what protects the prophet from falling into mistake in his legislation, given that he is a human whose nature is the nature of other individuals in the possibility of falling into error? It is well known that the occurrence of error in this stage—the stage of religion and the removal of discord from society—is equivalent to the social discord itself in blocking the path of the human species' perfection and misleading this species in its journey toward its felicity. Thus, the problem returns from the start!

**I say:** The previous discussions are sufficient to resolve this knot. For that which drove this species toward this actuality—meaning the spiritual matter that removes discord—is none other than the existential law (الناموس التكويني), which is the existential delivery (الايصال التكويني) of every species of existential types to its existential perfection and true felicity. For the cause that necessitated the existence of man in the external world as a real existence like other external species is the same that guides him with an external existential guidance to his felicity. It is well known that external matters, insofar as they are external, are not subject to error and mistake; that is, external existence does not contain error and mistake, for it is obvious that what is in the external world is what is in the external world! Error and mistake only occur in the sciences of judgment (العلوم التصديقية) and intellectual matters from the perspective of their application to the external world; for truth and falsehood are properties of propositions, occurring to them in terms of their correspondence to the external world or lack thereof. If it is assumed that what guides this species to its felicity and the removal of its discord occurring in its society is creation and existence (الايجاد والتكوين), it follows that no mistake or error occurs in its guidance, nor in the means of its guidance, which is the spirit of prophethood and the consciousness of revelation. Thus, creation does not err in placing this spirit and consciousness in the existence of the prophet, nor does this consciousness it placed err in identifying the interests of the species from its corruptions, and its felicity from its wretchedness. If we were to assume error and mistake in its affair, it would be necessary to rectify it with another matter protected from error and mistake. Thus, it is necessary that the matter of creation stands upon correctness (صواب) in which there is no error or mistake.

It has thus appeared that this prophetic spirit does not reside in a place except accompanied by infallibility (العصمة) — *the divinely granted protection from error and sin* — which is the protection from error in the matter of religion and the legislated law. This infallibility is other than the infallibility from disobedience (المعصية) as we indicated previously; for this is infallibility in receiving the revelation from Allah, the Glorified, while that is infallibility in the station of action and servitude. There is a third stage of infallibility, which is infallibility in delivering the revelation; for both are situated in the path of the existential felicity of man as an existential occurrence, and there is no error or mistake in creation.

From what we have mentioned, the answer to another problem in this context has appeared: Why is it not permissible for this internal consciousness to be like the shared primordial consciousness—meaning intellectual consciousness—in its mode of existence, by being subject to the law of change and influence? For intellectual consciousness, even if it is a non-material matter and among the things subsisting in the soul that is immaterial, yet from the perspective of its connection to matter, it accepts intensity and weakness, persistence and cessation, as in the cases of insanity, folly, idiocy, stupidity, the weakness of old age, and other ailments that befall the perceptive powers. Likewise, this internal consciousness is a matter linked to the material body in some way. Even if it is granted that it is non-material in its essence, its state must be like that of intellectual consciousness in accepting change and corruption. With the possibility of change and corruption occurring in it, the previous problems certainly return.

The answer: We have explained that this driving—meaning the driving of the human species toward its true felicity—is only realized by the hand of external fashioning and creation (الصنع والايجاد الخارجي) rather than the intellectual intellect. There is no meaning for the realization of error in external existence.

As for this internal consciousness being subject to change and corruption because it is linked in some way to the body, we do not grant that every consciousness linked to the body is subject to change and corruption. What is granted of that is only this intellectual consciousness (and it has already passed that prophetic consciousness is not of the genus of intellectual consciousness). This is because among consciousnesses is man's consciousness of himself, which does not accept cessation, corruption, change, or error; for it is presential knowledge (علم حضوري) — *knowledge by presence where the known is identical to the knowledge itself* — where the known is the very external known. The completion of this discussion is left to its proper place.

From what has passed, several matters have become clear:

**First:** The inclination of human society toward civilization and discord.

**Second:** That this discord, which cuts off the path of the species' felicity, is not removed and will never be removed by the laws established by the intellectual intellect.

**Third:** That the remover of this discord is none other than the prophetic consciousness that Allah, the Glorified, creates in some individuals of mankind.

**Fourth:** That the genus of this internal consciousness found in the prophets is other than the genus of the intellectual consciousness shared among the rational ones of the human species.

**Fifth:** That this internal consciousness does not err in its perception of the beliefs and laws that reform the state of the human species in its true felicity.

**Sixth:** That these results (and what concerns us among them are the last three: the necessity of sending prophets, the fact that the consciousness of revelation is of a different genus than intellectual consciousness, and that the prophet is infallible and does not err in receiving revelation) are results produced by the general law (الناموس العام) observed in this natural world. This is the journey of every one of the species observed therein toward its felicity through the guidance of the existential causes that equipped it with the means of journeying toward its felicity, reaching it, and being clothed in it. Man is one of these species, and he is equipped with that by which he can believe the true belief, be clothed in virtuous dispositions, and perform righteous deeds in a sound, virtuous city. Thus, existence must prepare for him this felicity one day in the external world and guide him to it with an existential guidance in which there is no error or mistake, according to what has been explained.
